1.1: This is the word Yahweh spoke to Hosea son of Beeri — during the years of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah in Judah, and in the days of Jeroboam son of Joash in Israel.
1.2: This was the very first word Yahweh spoke through Hosea: 'Go, marry a woman given to prostitution, and raise children of that same bent — for this land has been utterly faithless, chasing after other gods instead of me.'
1.3: Hosea obeyed. He married Gomer, the daughter of Diblaim, and she became pregnant and bore him a son.
1.4: Yahweh told him: 'Name the boy Jezreel — for soon I will call to account the house of Jehu for the blood spilled at Jezreel, and I will break the royal power of Israel.'
1.5: On that day I will shatter Israel's military might in the very valley that bears Jezreel's name.
1.6: Gomer conceived again and bore a daughter. God said: 'Name her Lo-ruhamah — Not Shown Mercy — for I will no longer extend mercy to Israel. I have reached the end of my patience with them.'
1.7: Judah, however, will receive my mercy. I will deliver them — but not by military force, not by bows or swords or cavalry. I, Yahweh their God, will rescue them.
1.8: Once Lo-ruhamah was weaned, Gomer became pregnant once more and gave birth to a son.
1.9: God said: 'Name him Lo-ammi — Not My People. For that is what you have become: not my people, and I will not be yours.'
1.10: And yet — the descendants of Israel will multiply beyond counting, like the sand of the seashore. In the very place where they were told 'You are not my people,' they will be called 'Children of the living God.'
1.11: Judah and Israel will be reunited. Together they will choose one leader and rise from the land of exile. That day — the day of Jezreel — will be magnificent.

2.1: Call your brothers 'My People' and your sisters 'My Beloved.' The names of rejection have been reversed.
2.2: Plead with her — plead with your mother, for I am no longer her husband, and she is no longer my wife. Let her put away her adultery, strip the tokens of her other lovers from her face and her body,
2.3: or I will strip her bare — as naked as the day she was born — and make her as desolate as a desert, as dry as cracked earth, and leave her to die of thirst.
2.4: I will show no mercy to her children either — they were born from her faithlessness.
2.5: Their mother has played the whore; she who bore them has shamed herself. She said: 'I will chase after my lovers — they are the ones who give me bread and water, wool and linen, oil and wine.'
2.6: So I will fence her in with thorns; I will build a wall around her so she cannot find the roads she ran down.
2.7: She will run after her lovers and not catch them; search for them and not find them. Then at last she will say: 'I will go back to my husband as before — my life was better then than now.'
2.8: She never grasped that it was I — Yahweh — who had given her the grain, the wine, the olive oil, who had enriched her with silver and gold. And she took all of it and offered it to Baal.
2.9: So I will take back what is mine: at harvest I will take the grain, at vintage the wine. I will reclaim the wool and the linen I gave to cover her — and she will stand exposed.
2.10: I will expose her shame in full view of her lovers, and not one of them will be able to save her from me.
2.11: I will silence every celebration: no more festivals, no more new moon gatherings, no more sabbaths, no more sacred assemblies. The joy will be stripped away.
2.12: I will lay waste to her vineyards and fig orchards — the ones she called 'wages my lovers paid me.' I will let them go wild; the animals of the open country will eat them down.
2.13: I will call her to account for every day she burned incense to the Baals — the days she dressed herself in rings and jewelry to chase her lovers — and forgot me completely. This is Yahweh's word.
2.14: And yet — I will woo her. I will lead her out into the wilderness and speak to her heart there.
2.15: From that wilderness I will give her vineyards again; I will transform the Valley of Achor — the Valley of Trouble — into a gateway of hope. She will sing for me there as she sang in her youth, as she sang on the day she came out of Egypt.
2.16: On that day — Yahweh declares — you will call me 'My Husband,' and never again 'My Baal.' The word itself will be changed.
2.17: I will purge the names of the Baals from her mouth — she will not even speak them, let alone worship them.
2.18: On that day I will establish a covenant with all creation — with the wild animals, the birds of the air, the creatures of the ground. I will shatter bows and swords and end war from the earth, and my people will lie down in safety.
2.19:
I will make you my bride forever —
I will betroth you with righteousness and justice,
with faithful love and tender compassion.
2.20:
I will betroth you to me in fidelity,
and you will truly know Yahweh — not as a name but as a living presence.
2.21:
On that day I will answer — Yahweh declares.
I will answer the heavens, and the heavens will answer the earth,
2.22:
the earth will answer the grain, the wine, and the olive oil,
and they will answer Jezreel — God Sows.
2.23:
I will plant her in the land like a seed sown by my own hand.
I will love the one I called Lo-ruhamah — Not Shown Mercy.
I will say to Lo-ammi — Not My People — 'You are my people.'
And they will answer: 'You are our God.'

3.1: Then Yahweh said to me: 'Go again — love a woman who is unfaithful, who is loved by another yet still commits adultery. Love her as I love Israel, even though they keep turning to other gods and lavishing devotion on empty things.'
3.2: So I bought her back — fifteen pieces of silver, and a homer and a half of barley. That was the price I paid to bring her home.
3.3: I told her: 'You will stay with me for a long time. No prostitution. No other man. And I — I will be faithful to you.'
3.4: This is what Israel's future holds: a long, suspended time — no king, no leader, no sacrifice, no sacred pillar, no priestly ephod, no household gods. A long waiting, stripped of all the props of both true and false religion.
3.5: But afterward — Israel will turn back. They will seek Yahweh their God and David their king. In the days to come, trembling with awe, they will come to Yahweh and receive his goodness.

4.1: Listen to the word of Yahweh, O Israel — for Yahweh has a legal case against the inhabitants of this land. There is no truth, no faithful love, no knowledge of God anywhere in it.
4.2: Cursing and deception, murder and theft and adultery are everywhere; lawlessness floods the land, and blood flows into blood without end.
4.3: The land itself is in mourning; everything on it is wasting away — the wild animals, the birds of the sky, and even the fish of the sea are being swept away. All creation suffers the covenant's breach.
4.4: Let no one point fingers or bring charges — for the whole people are as guilty as those who dare to contend against a priest.
4.5: You will stumble in broad daylight, and the prophets will stumble alongside you in the darkness. I will destroy the nation that bore you.
4.6: My people are perishing — not for lack of food, but for lack of knowledge of me. Because you have refused to know me, I will refuse to have you serve as my priests. Because you have forgotten the instruction of your God, I will forget your children.
4.7: The larger they grew, the deeper they sinned against me — so I will trade their glory for disgrace.
4.8: The priests feed off the people's sins — they actually profit from Israel's guilt, so they have no incentive to call anyone back to me.
4.9: Priest and people are equally corrupt — the same judgment will fall on both. I will repay each according to what they have done.
4.10: They will eat but never be full; they will chase after fertility rites but bear no fruit — because they have forsaken Yahweh.
4.11: Lust and liquor have stolen their minds.
4.12: My people seek guidance from a carved stick; a wooden rod is their oracle. A spirit that drives them to prostitution has made them wander — they have abandoned their God and gone after idols.
4.13: They climb to every hilltop and burn offerings under every shady tree — oak, poplar, terebinth — calling it sacred. The result? Your daughters are turning to prostitution, your daughters-in-law to adultery. The worship shapes the worshippers.
4.14: I will not single out your daughters for punishment, or your daughters-in-law — not when the men themselves are the ones slipping off to prostitutes and sacrificing with cult workers at the shrines. A people who do not understand will be brought to ruin.
4.15: Israel, your adultery is your own. Judah, do not follow. Stay away from Gilgal; do not go up to Beth-aven — that house of wickedness that once was Bethel. Do not take oaths in Yahweh's name there; the sanctuary is defiled.
4.16: Israel is as stubborn as a heifer that refuses the yoke. Will Yahweh now pasture them like a lamb in an open meadow? They are too wild for that mercy.
4.17: Ephraim has bound himself to his idols. Leave him.
4.18: Their drinking parties are a disgrace; they are given over completely to unfaithfulness. Their leaders love the shame of it — they cry out for more.
4.19: A wind will catch them up and carry them off — and when they land, their sacrifices will be shown for what they were: sources of their shame.

5.1: Listen to this — priests! Hear it — all Israel! Pay attention — royal house! The verdict is coming against you. You have laid a trap at Mizpah and stretched a net across Tabor to catch the people in your corruption.
5.2: The rebels are steeped in treachery; they have dug deep into slaughter — though I have been warning them all along.
5.3: I know Ephraim through and through; Israel holds nothing back from my sight. Ephraim, you have chosen prostitution — and all of Israel is contaminated by it.
5.4: The way they live makes it impossible to turn back. A spirit of prostitution has taken hold in the center of their being, and they have no real knowledge of Yahweh.
5.5: Israel's own pride stands as a witness against him — an open accusation. Israel and Ephraim will fall under the weight of their guilt, and Judah will fall alongside them.
5.6: They will bring all their animals to the sanctuaries, hunting for Yahweh — but they will not find him. He has withdrawn from their midst.
5.7: They have betrayed Yahweh; the children they have raised are strangers to him. A month — a single cycle — will be enough to consume them and everything they think is theirs.
5.8: Blow the war-horn at Gibeah! Sound the trumpet at Ramah! Raise the alarm at Beth-aven: 'Enemy behind you, Benjamin!'
5.9: The day of reckoning will leave Ephraim in ruins. Among all the tribes of Israel I have spoken the undeniable word.
5.10: Judah's leaders have pushed past every boundary like landgrabbers who move the property markers. I will pour out my fury on them like a flood.
5.11: Ephraim is crushed, ground down by the very justice he rejected — because he chose to follow empty human commands instead of God.
5.12: To Ephraim I will be like a moth quietly eating through the garment of their strength; to the house of Judah, like dry rot working through the timber of their security.
5.13: When Ephraim finally saw how sick he was, when Judah felt how deep the wound was — instead of turning to me, Ephraim ran to Assyria and appealed to the great king. But that king cannot heal you. He cannot cure what is wrong with you.
5.14: I will come against Ephraim like a lion, like a young lion against the house of Judah. I myself will seize them and go — carry them off — and no one will be able to take them back.
5.15: I will withdraw to my place and wait until they acknowledge what they have done and seek my face. When they are in desperate trouble — only then will they search for me with all their heart.

6.3:
Let us press on to know Yahweh.
His rising is as sure as the morning sun;
he will come to us like the rain,
like the spring rains that drench the earth.
6.4:
What am I to do with you, Ephraim?
What am I to do with you, Judah?
Your faithful love — your hesed — is like the morning fog:
it vanishes before the sun is fully up.
6.5:
That is why I have cut into you through the prophets,
struck you down with the words of my mouth —
so my judgment would break through like light through storm cloud.
6.6:
What I want is faithful love — hesed — not ritual sacrifice;
knowing me deeply, not the smoke of burnt offerings.
6.7:
But like Adam at the beginning, they broke the covenant —
there in the land of their blessing, they betrayed me.
6.8: Gilead — a city of wrongdoers, steeped in bloodguilt.
6.9:
The priests are bandits waiting in ambush:
they murder on the road to Shechem,
covering up every outrage with collusion.
6.11:
And you, Judah — a harvest is coming for you too,
when I turn and restore the fortunes of my people.

13.1: Once, when Ephraim spoke, the whole land trembled at his authority — he was the dominant force in Israel. But when he made himself guilty through Baal, he was finished.
13.2: Their sinning only deepens. They have smelted their silver into molten images — idols designed from their own imaginations, manufactured by hired craftsmen. And the priests tell the worshippers: 'Kiss the calves.' This is what Israelite worship has become.
13.3:
They will vanish —
like morning mist when the sun rises,
like dew gone by midmorning,
like chaff the wind scatters from the threshing floor,
like smoke that slips out through a window and is gone.
Nothing will remain of them.
13.4: But I — I am Yahweh your God, the one who brought you out of Egypt. You are to know no god but me. There is no deliverer but me. There never was.
13.5: In that wilderness — that burning, waterless land — I was the one who knew you and kept you alive.
13.6: I fed them; they were satisfied. Satisfaction became pride, and pride became forgetting. They forgot me. It is the oldest pattern in Israel's story.
13.7: So now I become their predator — a lion in their path, a leopard crouching in ambush by the road.
13.8: I will come at them like a mother bear whose cubs have been taken — nothing is more fierce. I will rip open their rib cages, lay bare their hearts, and devour them like a lion. Whatever is left, the beasts of the open country will finish.
13.9: Israel — you have done this to yourself. But I remain the only one who could have saved you.
13.10: Where is your king now? Where is he to save you in all your cities? Where are all the rulers you demanded — when you cried, 'Give us a king! Give us leaders!' — as if a human king could be your salvation?
13.11: I gave you a king in my rage — and in my fury I reclaimed him. The monarchy was never a gift; it was a concession to your rebellion.
13.12: Ephraim's guilt has been bound up, sealed, filed away — not forgotten, but kept. It waits for the day it will be unsealed.
13.13: The labor pains of childbirth will seize him. But Ephraim is a fool of a child: at the very moment he should emerge into new life, he refuses to come out. He cannot seize the moment that would remake him.
13.14: Will I rescue them from Death's grip — redeem them from Sheol? No. Death, bring your plagues! Sheol, unleash your destruction! I will show them no pity. — These words later ring through Paul's lips in 1 Corinthians 15 as a victory taunt over a defeated enemy; here, in Hosea's mouth, they are a terrible summons: God calling Death to do its work.
13.15: Ephraim's very name means Fruitful — and for a season he lived up to it. But an east wind is coming, rising out of the wilderness, driven by Yahweh himself. The sirocco dries every spring and shrivels every fountain. And the army that follows that wind will plunder every precious thing they own.
13.16: Samaria will bear her guilt — she has defied her God. They will die by the sword. Their babies will be dashed against the stones; their pregnant women will be cut open. This is what ancient conquest looked like, and Yahweh will not shield a people who have refused him.

14.1: Come back, Israel — come back to Yahweh your God. You have fallen because of your own sin; only he can raise you.
14.2: Come to Yahweh with words — not animals, but words. Say this to him: 'Lift every sin away from us. Receive us back in grace. We will pay you back with the only offering we have left — the praise that rises from our lips, our words of thanksgiving instead of calves on an altar.'
14.3: We will not look to Assyria to save us. We will not trust in cavalry or military power. We will never again bow to anything our own hands have made and call it God. For in you — you alone — the helpless find mercy, and the fatherless find a Father.
14.4: I will cure them of their turning-away — their persistent faithlessness. I will love them freely, without condition, without price. My anger is over. It has turned and gone.
14.5:
I will come to Israel like the morning dew — bringing life where there was only dust.
He will burst into flower like the lily
and drive his roots down deep as the cedars of Lebanon.
14.7:
All who shelter under his shade will come alive again —
growing like grain in a good year,
blooming like a flourishing vine.
And the name of Israel will carry a fragrance
like the fine wine that comes from Lebanon's vineyards.
14.8:
Then Ephraim will say at last: 'What do I want with idols anymore?'
And Yahweh answers: 'I am the one who answers you; I am the one who has been watching over you all along.
I am like an ever-green cypress —
and every fruit you bear has always come from me.'
14.9:
Whoever is wise — let him take these words to heart.
Whoever has understanding — let him grasp what has been said here.
Yahweh's ways are straight and right:
the righteous walk in them and live,
but those who refuse to turn will stumble and fall in the very same path.

7.1: Just as I moved to heal Israel, Ephraim's guilt lay bare before me — and Samaria's wickedness with it. Every attempt at healing only exposes the rot: thieves break in, gangs plunder in the open.
7.2: What they never think about is this: I have not forgotten a single one of their wrongs. Now those deeds have closed in around them like a crowd — they stand there in full view before me.
7.3: They buy their king's approval with villainy and win their princes with lies. The court that should correct them only rewards them.
7.4: The whole lot of them are adulterers — burning with their lust like an oven that tends itself: once the dough is set, the baker doesn't need to stir the coals; it heats itself until the leaven does its work.
7.5: On the day of the royal feast, the princes made themselves sick with wine — and the king himself joined in with a crowd of mockers. The royal celebration became a revel of shame.
7.6: All through the night, while their baker sleeps, their plotting hearts smolder like an oven. Come morning, the fire breaks into full flame — the conspiracy is ready to act.
7.7: They blaze like an oven — and they have consumed their judges, brought down king after king. Not one of them has ever called out to me.
7.8: Ephraim has dissolved himself into the nations — he is like a pancake left unturned on the griddle: burnt black on one side, raw dough on the other. Half-ruined, and he doesn't even know it.
7.9: Foreign powers have been sapping his strength, and he hasn't noticed. Gray is spreading through his hair, and he still doesn't see it. Israel is aging and failing without awareness.
7.10: Israel's own arrogance stands as an open accusation against him — he cannot deny it. And still — even with all this happening to them — they refuse to return to Yahweh their God or seek him.
7.11: Ephraim is like a foolish dove that flutters this way and that with no sense of direction — crying out to Egypt, then flying off to Assyria. Any power but me.
7.12: As they fly off, I will cast my net over them and bring them down like birds from the sky. I will discipline them — just as the warnings they have long heard foretold.
7.13: Grief for them — they have run from me! Ruin waits for them — they have defied me! I was ready to redeem them, and they repaid me with lies.
7.14: They cry out, but not to me — just wailing on their beds in self-pity. They assemble to pray for better harvests, for grain and wine — and in their hearts they are still in rebellion against me.
7.15: I was the one who shaped them, who gave their arms their strength — and they turned that same strength to plotting against me.
7.16: They come back — but not to me, not to the Most High. They are like a bow that misfires when you most need it. Their princes will die by the sword for what their tongues have bragged, and Egypt will hold them in contempt.

8.1: Sound the alarm! Lift the trumpet to your mouth — an eagle is diving on the house of Yahweh. They brought this on themselves by shattering my covenant and defying my law.
8.2: They cry out to me: 'Our God! We know you — we are Israel!' But the cry is hollow. Knowledge of me is the very thing they lack.
8.3: Israel has rejected what is good — and now an enemy will hunt him down.
8.4: They have crowned kings without consulting me, installed princes without my knowledge. They took the silver and gold I gave them and hammered it into idols — idols that can only bring them to ruin.
8.5: Samaria, your golden calf has been thrown back in your face. My anger is blazing — how long before they are even capable of being clean?
8.6: That calf was made in Israel — a craftsman's hands shaped it. It is not God. The calf of Samaria will be smashed to splinters.
8.7: They have sown the wind — and a whirlwind is the harvest they will get. No grain in the stalk, no flour in the bud. And even if anything grew, a foreign army would eat it.
8.8: Israel has been swallowed up — absorbed among the nations, worth no more than a broken pot that no one wants to keep.
8.9: Ephraim has bolted off to Assyria like a wild donkey wandering on its own — and paid good money for foreign alliances, purchasing what God would have given freely.
8.10: Even now, while they are out buying allies among the nations, I will gather them in — but they will groan under the tribute demands of kings and princes. The very alliances they paid for will crush them.
8.11: Ephraim has built altar after altar — but every altar was built for sin, and every altar has only generated more of the same. Worship that was never toward me becomes a machine for self-corruption.
8.12: I wrote out my law for them in full — the whole abundant gift of it — and they treated it like a strange document from a foreign country. Not their law. Not their God.
8.13: They bring their offerings and eat the meat — but Yahweh takes no pleasure in any of it. Now their guilt will be remembered; now their sins will be reckoned with. Back to Egypt they will go — back to the house of bondage.
8.14: Israel has forgotten who made him — and has been building temples to show off. Judah has spent the same energy fortifying city after city. But I will send fire on all those cities, and the strongholds they trusted in will burn.

9.1: Stop celebrating, Israel — you have no right to rejoice the way other nations do. You have played the whore against your God, chasing after the fertility-rite wages on every threshing floor where the grain piled up.
9.2: The very grain floors and wine vats they prostituted themselves at will not sustain them. The new wine will fail.
9.3: The land of Yahweh will no longer be their home. Ephraim will go back to Egypt; in Assyria they will eat what the law forbids — exiled from the land, cut off from the altar.
9.4: Exiled, they will not be able to bring their wine offerings to Yahweh; no sacrifice they offer will delight him. Their sacred food will become like bread eaten at a funeral — anyone who touches it is defiled. Their bread will feed only their own bodies; none of it will reach the house of Yahweh.
9.5: When the feast day comes — the solemn assembly, the pilgrimage festival — what will you do? You will be in exile. There is no feast in a foreign land.
9.6: They flee to escape ruin — and Egypt will be their grave. Memphis will bury them. The treasured houses they left behind will be overtaken by nettles; thornbushes will fill the rooms where they once lived.
9.7: The day of reckoning has arrived — the day of payback is here. Israel will feel it. But they call the prophet a fool and the man of the Spirit a madman. This is what their iniquity and contempt for God's word has done to them: they mock the messenger because they cannot bear the message.
9.8: The prophet was supposed to be Ephraim's watchman — stationed beside me, God. Instead he has become a bird-trap set on every path, and his own sanctuary is riddled with hatred.
9.9: They have plunged into the depths of corruption — as bad as the days of Gibeah, when Israel reached its lowest point. God has not forgotten; he will call every sin to account.
9.10: I remember finding Israel in the wilderness — it was like coming upon grapes in a desert, like seeing the very first ripe figs of the season on a young tree. What a find that was. But they went to Baal-peor and surrendered themselves to the shameful rites there, and became as abominable as the idol they embraced.
9.11: Ephraim's glory will take wing and vanish like a startled bird — no more children born, no more pregnancies, no more new life conceived. The fruit of the womb is taken away.
9.12: Even if they manage to raise their children, I will take those children too — not one will remain. And when I finally withdraw from them — when that departure comes — it will be their true catastrophe.
9.13: I once saw Ephraim as planted in a fertile, well-watered place — like Tyre in its glory. But now Ephraim is leading his own children out to the executioner.
9.14: What is left to pray for, Yahweh? Give them — what can you give them now? Give them wombs that lose the child, and breasts that run dry. Let the line stop here.
9.15: Gilgal is where it all came to a head — that is where I first turned against them. Because of what they do, I will expel them from my household. I will love them no longer. Every one of their rulers is a rebel.
9.16: Ephraim has been struck. The root has gone dry — no fruit will come from this tree. And if in spite of it all a child is born, I will take that beloved child too. The line ends here.
9.17: My God will throw them away — they would not listen. And so they will wander, rootless, among the nations, with no land to call their own.

10.1: Israel was a thriving vine — plenty of fruit. But every time the harvest grew, he built more altars to his idols; every time the land was good to him, he improved the sacred pillars. Prosperity did not lead to gratitude — it fed more idolatry.
10.2: Their hearts have been playing both sides — and now comes the reckoning. God will tear down their altars and smash their sacred stones.
10.3: They are already saying it: 'We have no king.' Of course not — they never truly feared Yahweh, and without that, a human king is worthless. They are learning the hard way what it means to have abandoned the only King who could help them.
10.4: Their mouths are full of fine-sounding agreements — but every oath is false, every treaty is hollow. The result? Justice has grown up like hemlock in their fields — bitter, toxic, choking out what was meant to grow.
10.5: The people of Samaria are going to dread what happens to the golden calf at Beth-aven — Hosea's name for what was once Bethel, now a house of wickedness. They will mourn over the idol; the priests who built careers on it and reveled in its glory will wail when the glory is stripped away.
10.6: That very calf will be hauled off to Assyria as a tribute payment to the great king. Ephraim will carry the shame of it; Israel will blush for the strategy that led to this.
10.7: Samaria's king will vanish — gone in a moment, like a chip of bark swept off in the current.
10.8: The high places of Beth-aven — the shrine that became a den of sin — will be demolished. Thorns and thistles will take over the altar mounds. And in their terror, the people will cry out to the mountains: 'Fall on us! Hide us!' Better to be buried than to face what is coming.
10.9: Israel, you have been on this path since the days of Gibeah — that ancient catastrophe when evil planted itself and no one drove it out. Is war not going to catch up with the sons of such wickedness?
10.10: I will choose the moment when I come against them in discipline. The nations will assemble against them when I punish them — doubly — for sins stacked on sins.
10.11: Ephraim was like a heifer that loved the easy work of threshing — grain scattered on the floor, no heavy labor. But I am putting a yoke on that beautiful neck. Ephraim will pull a plow; Judah will furrow the hard ground; Jacob will break the clods. The easy days are over.
10.12:
Sow righteousness — that is what the ground needs now.
Reap the harvest of faithful love;
break open the hardened soil of your hearts.
It is time — high time — to seek Yahweh,
until he comes and rains his righteousness down on you.
10.13: But that is not what you have done. You plowed wickedness and reaped injustice — you have been eating the harvest of your own lies. You put your confidence in your strategies, in the size of your armies —
10.14: — and so war will erupt among your people; every fortress will be torn down, the way Shalman tore down Beth-arbel in battle, leaving mothers and children alike crushed together in the rubble.
10.15: Bethel — the very center of Israel's false religion — will be the agent of their ruin. At the break of dawn, in one swift stroke, the king of Israel will be finished.

11.1:
When Israel was a child, I loved him —
and out of Egypt I called my son.
11.3:
I was the one who taught Ephraim to walk,
taking him by the arms —
but they never understood that it was I who healed them.
11.4:
I led them with human tenderness, with ropes of love.
I was the one who lifted the yoke from their jaws
and bent down to feed them.
11.5: They will not escape to Egypt — the Assyrian will be their king instead, because they refused to turn back to me.
11.6: The sword will fall on city after city, consuming and devouring — and their own plans are what brought it down on them.
11.7: My people are committed to walking away from me. They may cry upward — but their hearts never truly go there. No one among them actually lifts the Most High up.
11.8:
How can I give you up, Ephraim?
How can I hand you over, Israel?
How can I make you like Admah,
treat you the way Zeboiim was treated?
My heart is turning over inside me;
my compassion is rising, tender and warm.
11.9:
I will not act on the heat of my anger;
I will not turn again and destroy Ephraim.
For I am God — not a human being.
I am the Holy One in your midst,
and I will not come against you in fury.
11.11:
They will flutter home like birds from Egypt,
like doves from the land of Assyria —
and I will place them back in their own homes.
This is the word of Yahweh.
11.12:
Ephraim encircles me with nothing but lies;
Israel's house wraps itself in deceit.
But Judah still walks with God,
still faithful to the Holy One.

12.1: Ephraim gorges on wind — he chases the burning east wind all day long, filling his life with lies and ruin. He cuts deals with Assyria on one side and sends tribute oil to Egypt on the other. Every alliance is a betrayal.
12.2: Yahweh has a legal case against Judah too — and will call Jacob to account for everything he has done, paying back deed for deed.
12.3: From the very womb, Jacob was grabbing — he seized his brother by the heel. And when he grew strong, he wrestled with God himself.
12.4: He fought the angel and won — but then he wept and begged for a blessing. It was at Bethel that he found God, and there God spoke a word that has echoed ever since — a word spoken to us as well.
12.5:
The LORD God of Hosts — that is the name.
That is who met Jacob at Bethel.
Yahweh: the name that endures forever.
12.6:
So then — return to your God.
Practice faithful love and do what is just.
Wait for your God, always.
12.7: Ephraim has become a trader — dishonest scales in hand, delighted to exploit. This is what he has reduced himself to.
12.8: Ephraim says: 'Look how rich I've become — I earned all of this myself. No one can charge me with wrongdoing. Everything I have is clean profit.' The self-justification of the successful sinner.
12.9: But I am Yahweh your God — the one who brought you out of Egypt. I will make you live in tents again, the way you did in the wilderness — stripped of the wealth you are so proud of, back to the beginning.
12.10: I spoke — through the prophets again and again. I multiplied the visions. I used images and comparisons to reach them. The word was never absent. They were never without warning.
12.11: Gilead — nothing but wickedness. Worthless, every bit of it. In Gilgal they keep piling up their sacrifices to bulls; their altars will become nothing but heaps of rubble in the furrows — relics of a ruined religion.
12.12: Remember: Jacob ran — fled to Aram. He served years for a wife, years of labor to win the woman he loved.
12.13: Through a prophet — Moses — Yahweh brought Israel out of Egypt. Through that same prophet Israel was preserved. The word of God given through human servants: that is how God works.
12.14: Ephraim has provoked Yahweh to the bitter end. So Yahweh will let the blood Ephraim has shed remain on him — and every act of shame will be thrown back in his face.
